turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The issue brought before the Court today in these consolidated cases is whether or not constitutional due process of law requires a hearing outside the presence of the jury to determine the admissibility of -- identification evidence. We submit that the issue before this Court is not the narrow one concerning the admission of identification evidence, but rather the issue, and the essence of that issue goes to effective assistance of counsel. The Court must examine the practical application of the issue because it has a direct effect on the practice of criminal law in cases throughout this country. The Petitioners will demonstrate to the Court that

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Yes, be it a show-up, line-up, or --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or a proposed identification in Court?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: That's correct, Justice Stewart. The --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What you're saying is that cross-examina­ tion is not sufficient?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Exactly. We feel that, this Court has examined in its history, numerous cases involving identifica­ tion testimony. Those cases, to-wit, Wade, Stovall and Gil­ bert, were concerned with the suggestive identification procedures that may be implemented. and the Manson decisions, deal with the reliability of the identification evidence. The emphasis has, in fact, shifted. The present stand of the Manson standard, which

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There was not a line-up in Biggers, was

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: No, just the show-up. The question becomes how does due process require the Manson analysis to be implemented. And we submit there are several considerations along this line. First of all, as the Respondent argues here, that discovery is an adequate method by which to implement the Manson analysis. We submit that discovery is totally inade­ quate; primarily because the purpose of discovery is only to through a revision of its discovery rules. And we feel for achieve a reliable identification. The respondent also argues that the defense attorney has equal access to pretrial interviews of witnesses and that this will satisfy any constitutional requirements --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Counsel, did you make and preserve this right to counsel argument as opposed to the Wade, Stovall, Denno argument all through the Kentucky courts into the District Court in the Sixth Circuit?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Justice Rehnquist, I don't think anyone ever questioned the individual's right to counsel. We are not saying that the individual is entitled to counsel in pre- critical stages; that's not the issue. We assume, for example in Watkins' case, Watkins was identified in a line-up and a show-up before his indictment. We're not saying that he had a right to counsel at those points in time, all we're saying in this brief, is that counsel did have the right to an objected at the -- in trial, did you?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And did you ask at the time when the jury?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Pretrial. The judge called the case for trial, both attorneys asked for in camera hearings. Both attorneys in the Watkins and the Summitt cases cited Wade, Stovall and Gilbert and constitutional principles, to justify the request for the hearings.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You still haven't answered Justice Rehnquist's --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did they cite Jackson against Denno, or --

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: No. No one cited Jackson v. Denno.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that you didn't raise the point in the state court?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: It was raised and it was considered by, particularly, the Sixth Circuit. specifically raised.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well why did the federal habeas court think that you had exhausted your state remedies on this par­

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Well the Kentucky Supreme Court just found, in both of these cases, that there was no due process right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: To what, to what?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: To have the in camera hearing before eye witness identification could be admitted.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, so you did raise it in the state court?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: That specific issue was raised.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but was it -- on the grounds of what? Why did you think you were entitled to an in camera hearing?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Due process. Because the attorney on cross-examination cannot adequately challenge the reliability

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You've now embellished it with this, it's really a right to counsel, due process argument, is that it?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: It has certain ramifications for effective assistance of counsel, that's our position.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well the evidence was admitted, wasn't it?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you don't challenge that it wasn't admissible? Or do you say that if counsel had the unrestrained right to cross-examination, it might not have been?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: That's the issue to be determined and Court has to analyze the issue presented here from the per­ going to cross-examine a witness, for example, in the Summitt case, he's going to cross-examine a rape victim who is still emotionally distraught because of this experience over almost consideration what did the prosecutor achieve on direct examination. The Watkins case is a classic example. The pro­ secution didn't even touch the pretrial identification --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The rule you're advocating would apply, I take it to, as equally, to six witnesses, identification witnesses or 12, as it does to one, would it not?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it would apply outside the identifi­ cation field, I would take it, because a trial attorney has to make that sort of determination you're talking about, how much did the prosecutor accomplish on direct, with respect to every single witness in the case, whether to decline cross-examina­ tion, make an extensive cross-examination, or the like? economy to conduct that hearing, for several reasons. First of all, the attorney is going to -- if, assuming the reliabil­ ity is very strong, an in camera hearing may result in settle­ strong, the attorney, he may waive cross-examination of that witness and certainly, he's going to restrict and limit his cross-examination. He's not going to engage in the rambling type of cross-examination that Summitt's attorney engaged in. Summitt --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So you're saying, in effect, that the Constitution requires that there be this in camera or pretrial exploration so that the attorney can decide whether he wants to cross-examine and if so, how much; isn't that what it adds up to?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: It adds up to a question of the effective assistance of counsel, and --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, just answer my question. Isn't that what you're asking for? the constitutional ramifications of suggestive identification procedures and unreliability of identification evidence. In your hypothetical, Justice Rehnquist, the matter of whether or not a certain substance may be cocaine or heroin, that -- I don't see any constitutional problems with a denial of an in camera hearing to determine whether or not the substance is in fact a controlled substance. But here there are just far- reaching constitutional ramifications that require the hearing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well you're talking about reliability?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Exactly. But I would -- says that the government has todielp you in your case?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: I don't know if it's so much --there's nothing --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What is there in the Constitution that says that the government should do what you should do yourself as defense counsel?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: I think the Sixth Amendment requires the hearing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That the government should do what you ought to do yourself?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: The Constitution requires the government to do it. It doesn't require what the defense attorney wants it to --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well what in the Constitution says that

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Rape and robbery?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: It's only restricted to cases in which identification evidence is going to be introduced

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Suppose it's an SEC case for identifica- tion?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: I'm sorry, I don't follow the analogy. I don't --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Neither do I, I don't follow where you are either. See, that's my trouble. I just don't understand why this is singled out, except that this is the case that you have --

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Well, --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- that's the only reason it's singled eous conclusion. Because the analysis in Jackson went right to the practical effect of allowing -- what happens when the jury is allowed to determine the voluntariness of the confes­ sion, and pass on the defendant's guilt or innocence?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but the jury is not permitted to pass on the admissibility of evidence. of the evidence, and the reliability is based first of all, on, perhaps, suggestive identification procedures. That's -- has to be, whether or not the identification procedure is suggestive has to be determined. We submit that that's a question of law, given the decisions of this Court along these lines. After the Court decides whether or not the identification is suggestive, the suggestive procedures, is the identification reliable and can the jury itself pass on the credibility of the witness and the reliability of that identification. The problem I see, that this Court should take into account, is if we don't have the in camera hearing, what happens when the jury is confronted with the Foster v. Cali­ fornia situation? In which the identification procedures are obviously suggestive, the identification of the witness is obviously unreliable. What's the jury going to do in that situation? Is the jury, from the standpoint of human nature, going to be able to follow the trial judge's admonition to ignore any evidence concerning identification --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean, when it's -- after the hearing before the jury the evidence is excluded by the judge, he

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you suggest that ever, under any circumstances, that the witness who is tendered as an identi­ fication witness could . be excluded? reasons ? be any indication of suggestive identification procedures.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well if I understood your argument that you've been making, it is that you are entitled, as a consti­ tutional matter, to have some private sort of examination of

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: No, we don't subscribe to that position That in order to determine the --- whether or not those pro­

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, go back to the Chief Justice's question, if you will. You simply have a man named as a witness, he gets on the stand and begins testifying about how he was in the bank at the time. He doesn't, no one asks him whether he has previously identified the witness, and then he is simply asked the question, do you recognize the defendant and was he in the bank. Now do you say you're entitled to an in camera hearing before that?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: I don't necessarily think, Your Honor, if there has been no indication that the state has not engaged in suggestive identification procedures. I'm not suggesting tification procedures. We submit that when those circumstances says yes, I saw that man in the bank, and the question then asked the witness, did you previously identify him, right after the robbery. Now, would you make a distinction between those two?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Yes, I think I would.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that, your Jackson against Denno hear­ ing would require it only where a witness is asked about a proceeding out of court identification?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Yes, such as presented here with these two cases. I don't think every witness in every case has to Only in those instances where the state has implemented identification procedures; the determination has to be made whether those state sanctioned procedures are suggestive, and then reliability is determined--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well let me understand you, sir. If the victim comes to the trial and says, that's the man, pointing to the accused sitting at counsel table, no out of court pro­ ceeding is required in that situation?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Assuming that there has been no other --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I've given you the hypothetical. The victim comes and testifies, as most do, I take it, that that man robbed me, and so forth --

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- and as to that testimony, no out of court proceeding is required, is that it?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: That's correct. .QUESTION: It's required only if the witness gets on the stand and says, I identified this man at a show-up on such and such a date at such and such a place or at a line-up. In that circumstance, before he can repeat the identification in Court, there has to be a proceeding such as you suggest, is that it?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: That's correct, Justice Brennan. That's our position.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, in Watkins, the -- all the prosecu­ tion relied on, as I understand it, was the in court identi­ fication?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then, on cross-examination, the defense brought out pretrial matters.

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: That's correct, Justice White. up and -- when does your right attach?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: I think as soon as --- I think the right attaches as soon as the police or the government authority

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean, before court, before trial?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: As long as, just before the identification evidence is admitted, whether it is before trial or after the commencement of trial, we would suggest that it be done before trial.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, but you say that -- I'm not exactly sure how much your response to Mr. Justice Brennan's hypothet­ ical eliminates. Is it in any case where an identification witness has previously talked to a police officer or to a pro­ secuting attorney?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: No sir. If in the course of the pro­ secuting attorney's examination, if he goes to that witness and says tell me what you saw and the witness says I saw that man rob the bank or I saw that man come out of that house with a t.v., I don't think the due process right would necessarily attach to challenge the reliability of the identification witness identify the defendant before trial, you have this due process right; well that would be always.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you haven't.

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: I don't think that it --in the situation that Justice Brennan gave, you're always entitled. You are implemented --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well won't there always be a pretrial identification of some kind?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Yes. But whether or not the state has undertaken any procedures which may have suggested or may have impaired the reliability of the identification, that's the issue to be determined. neighbor, . some neighbor says I saw him, and I know my neighbor very well -- MR-HEFT: Certainly, the next-door neighbor --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Heft, suppose the policeman just goes out and picks up a man at random and charges him? You would not have a right to go into that, would you? I'm assuming they picked up the wrong man. You wouldn't have a right then to have the hearing, would you? Would you?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: No, it does not --no. I think cross- examination --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does that cut you any way at all?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Our only position is that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That man is rather innocent.

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: I think cross-examination in that par­ ticular respect :--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it is true, isn't it, most states allow this kind of pretrial hearing?

turns.name: FRANK W. HEFT, JR.
turns.text: Well most, a number of states follow Kentucky's approach, in which the Kentucky Supreme Court said in both of these cases, this in camera hearing was a preferred course of action. There are also numerous jurisdictions that aspect, we would -- we don't agree with the application in the Driber case. We would submit that if you're going to have the hearing to determine whether or not your motion is frivolous, the evidence is going to come out anyway. And due process would require the hearing just on motion alone. I would just like to make a few concluding remarks. The issue, in recent years, the defense bar has come under argument before standing up here. to go in and check the alibi witnesses, the -- you know, is the defendant going to make this statement this time, or is ask it at trial, if it's not an incriminating statement, if it's to his advantage, I won't ask it as trial counsel.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Attorney General Fox, isn't there another consideration which you at least think about in a case like this? In this case, of course, the testimony is found to be admissible. But supposing you had show-up testimony that went in before the jury and after it went in, the judge thought it

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And is it not true that if inadmissible sionhearing would be to avoid that kind of prejudice?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: There is an alternative to that. Upon the judge declaring that evidence inadmissible, it would be no different than him declaring hearsay inadmissible, or any other evidence inadmissible. Trial counsel, defense counsel, at that point in time, asks for an admonition, if not given sua sponte, and in addition, he may ask for a mistrial. Admittedly, the prosecutor runs the burden, or runs the risk that if his iden­ tification is inadmissible everything is going to go down the tubes at that point in time. But assuming -- going on with your hypothetical, that the trial court denies the motion for mistrial, we still have the appellate review of due process, the fundamental unfair­ ness aspect; it does not require a per se constitutional requirement that we determine all these factors prior to trial.

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: It can go both ways, Your Honor. He could in, or it could be on a question by question basis. The fact In the rape case in Sununitt, the motion to suppress was based .on the fact that counsel was not present at the photographic viewing. In Watkins, the basic objection was lack of counsel at the line-up. The line-up was conducted, it was a pre-indictment line-up. The basic motions presented to these two separate trial courts was right to counsel at the initial identification procedure. These motions were presented outside the hearing of the jury. The Court took a look at what was available, in fact, I believe in the Summitt case, it was an off the record determination, there were some comments off the record. And the Court said no, the cases are, and there is no right to counsel at a photographic -- especially when the individual had gone through some 1200 photographs and mug shots before identifying Mr. Summitt. The Court said there is no suggestiveness here, that identification would be admissible. He had made the determination. started, or something like that, and you had to know the facts exist, had been preserved. Under Kentucky practice, would you trial decide the question of fact, or would you have a pretrial suppression hearing?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: Normally there would be a motion to suppress, based on those grounds; there would be conduct of a hearing to determine that particular issue. In other words, there would be --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then 'there’s the point in this case, that the evidence was clearly admissible and therefore there was no need for the hearing. But if there were legitimate differences of opinion as to the admissibility of the allegedly suggestive identification, would you then say that the Constitution would require a pretrial hearing?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: If there were sufficient -- I think it would be --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well in the case I gave you, say, I say you say you think they would allow hearing, would you say the identified in Court without any reference to the pretrial. The victim was -- identified, and there was no pretrial iden­

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm sorry, Mr. Fox, did I understand your brother to say that Kentucky has now adopted a rule governing this question?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: No, Your Honor, in fact, not to my know­ ledge .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Perhaps I misunderstood him •

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: Normally, in our jurisdiction, a motion to suppress -- there is, it's made pretrial, and the courts go into it. These are --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I didn't hear you.

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: I said normally there is a motion made ceeding? I mean, is evidence taken, or what? There was no --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I guess you have to come prepared to put on evidence, if you have to?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Should you be?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: You should be, and basically, when they do take the evidence is that they put on -- a police officer who either took the photographs out and showed them or con­ ducted the line-up, or something of this nature. And it shows that the identification procedure itself was not suggestive. Does not get into the person who actually made the identifica­ tion. We're talking here not --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well are there instances in which the trial judge in a suppression hearing will say yes, I find that it was suggestive? ticular factual situation. Needless to say, in one instance admissible, it will go to the jury; if I rule it inadmissible, I'll move for a mistrial. I'll entertain a motion for mistrial and direct a verdict of acquittal.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do I understand you to say that in Ken­ tucky practice, there is always a pretrial motion available to suppress either identification or other evidence?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. The motion to suppress is basically a routine motion filed if there is evidence which has been seized, either a Fourth Amendment right or identifi­ cation procedure.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does your practice require giving a list of witnesses to the defense in advance of the trial?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: Witnesses are available. On our pretrial discovery motion, scientific evidence, statements of witnesses are not available from the state. Witnesses are available, names are available. whatever the defense counsel has to say at that time, and is it a factual determination?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: It's the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Will they call witnesses at the hearing?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: There can be, it's not required. It's as the commentary to Federal Rule 104 says, that in the best court. It's not a per se constitutional rule that such a hear­ ing can determine', or determine the admissibility of the evidence. I'd like to point out basically the difference in Jackson, or that we perceive to be the difference in Jackson, is that in Jackson there was a statute which required the jury to determine the voluntariness of a confession. This Court sai that that could not be, the same jury determined the voluntari­ ness as well as the credibility of it, as -- and the guilt and innocence. We would submit that in this particular instance, there is no requirement that the jury determine the admissi­ bility of this evidence; the court, trial court, still makes ground?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Under Kentucky practice, is that correct?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: That is correct. And if it should perhaps get through our system, and on a habeas action, it would probably not get past that stage.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And basically I take it your, or the state's ca.se, is that it is the function of cross-examination to expose the flaws in any testimony, or flaws in the cred­ ibility of any witness?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: Right, credibility is always an issue for the jury. The credibility issue as to the identification, the witness' firmness in the identification is for the jury. And these are probably the only two cases that I'm aware of in Kentucky in which there were no -- there was no pretrial hearing. They came about a year apart and came out of the same jurisdiction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fox, suppose in these cases, the -- let's say in the Watkins' case, the prosecution witness is about to testify and identify, making an in court identifica­ tion and the defense objects and says this is a tainted identification, I'd like a hearing outside the presence of

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why would you grant judgment notwith­ standing the verdict simply because of trial error? Wouldn't you grant a motion for new trial?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: We have both in Kentucky, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I realize you have both, but aren't they generally posited on different grounds?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: I really do not know, it depends, I think personally, upon the counsel. If he Is moving for a judgment, or directed verdict of acquittal prior to the jury verdict, he comes back, he moves for a judgment notwithstanding the verdict I think those are identical, the motion for new trial --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's just a question of time?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then the motion for new trial simply says that there was some procedural unfairness or evidence admitted that may not have been reliable, and the defendant ought not to be sentenced on the basis of the first trial, but nonetheless, there was sufficient evidence to go to the jury?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: A motion for a new trial is still within the discretion of the trial court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But a motion for directed verdict of acquittal says there simply was not enough evidence to persuade a reasonable juror to convict, doesn't it?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: Our motion for directed verdict of acquit­ tal must be made before it is given to the jury. At the close of all evidence. If that is overruled and it goes to the jury and a verdict of guilty comes back, then the judgment o.v. can be made.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And those are the same standards?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: Basically yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Which is basically the sufficiency of the evidence?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And a motion for a new trial would basically be some error that occurred during the trial, having nothing to do with his ultimate innocence or guilt, or the sufficiency of the evidence •

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The judge's motion, if the judge decided at the end of the case, after the verdict, to enter a judgment notwithstanding the verdict, he would do that only if he de­ cided that, excluding the challenged evidence as to which he had instructed the jury to disregard, there was no evidence upon which a reasonable jury could return a verdict of guilty, that would be the process of the judge's thinking, would it not?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: Well, we basically have a standard that where it would clearly not be unreasonable for the jury to reach a verdict of guilty.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But if he was in doubt on --

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: He'd probably deny it and let it go up on appeal.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or order a new trial?

turns.name: VICTOR FOX
turns.text: He'd probably still let it go up on appeal, and let the appellate court, since we have elected judges, CERTIFICATE North American Reporting hereby certifies that the attached, pages represent an accurate transcript of electronic sound recording of the oral argument before the Supreme Court of the United States in the matter of: and that these pages constitute the original transcript of the proceedings for the records of the Court.
